narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic performance and test scores to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 29, full-time enrollment plans, and low decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 grades—and predicted a 71.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar based on historical patterns. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, signaling weaker performance relative to peers at a critical stage. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile ranking, also at 1 compared to an average of about 5.7, which further reinforced concerns about early academic struggles. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, lower than the average of 3.21, did pull slightly in the positive direction by suggesting some baseline capability, it wasn't enough to offset the top two concerns. Your LSAT score of 29, far below the dataset average of 36.7, and middle family income bracket played supporting roles but weren't the primary drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles and have stronger test scores, your profile aligns more closely with those who historically struggled on the bar. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal qualities, but rather how these metrics stacked up against patterns in the data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and perhaps exploring part-time options if full-time study has been a challenge. Building a stronger academic narrative could help in reapplying to schools with more flexible criteria or even community colleges for foundational law prep. With targeted improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor for future opportunities.
